16:1: The moon's orbit is tilted at an angle of 5 degrees relative to the ecliptic (the plane in which the sun appears to travel). The two orbits intersect at two points: the ascending node ("head") and the descending node ("tail").
16:2: The ascending node moves 3 minutes and 11 seconds per day in the reverse direction (retrograde) along the zodiac. This means the intersection points between the moon's orbit and the ecliptic slowly drift backward.
16:3: To calculate the position of the ascending node, start from a known epoch value and add the appropriate remainder based on the number of elapsed days. Since the node moves in retrograde, the remainders are in the reverse direction.
16:4: The remainders for the ascending node are pre-calculated for one day, ten days, one hundred days, one thousand days, one month, and one year -- exactly like the remainders for the moon's and sun's positions.
16:5: The position of the descending node is always 180 degrees opposite the ascending node. If the ascending node is at 50 degrees, the descending node is at 230 degrees. No separate calculation is needed -- just compute the ascending node and add 180.
16:6: To find the position of the ascending node at a given time: start from the epoch value, add the remainders for years, months, and days, and the result is the node's position at the desired moment.
16:7: The latitude of the moon is its distance north or south of the ecliptic (the sun's apparent path). When the moon is exactly on the ecliptic (at the ascending or descending node), its latitude is zero. At its farthest point (90° from the node), the latitude reaches its maximum of 5°. The diagram shows the ecliptic as a horizontal line, with the moon above it (north) or below it (south), and the nodes at each end.
16:8: To find the moon's latitude, calculate the distance between the moon's true longitude and the nearest node. This distance determines how far the moon is from the ecliptic.
16:9: If the moon is between the ascending node and the descending node in the upper half, it is north of the ecliptic. If it is in the lower half (from the descending node back to the ascending node), it is south. The direction matters for calculating the moon's visibility.
16:10: The latitude table gives the moon's distance from the ecliptic for every 10 degrees, from 10 to 90. For example: at 10 degrees from the node the latitude is about 52 minutes; at 90 degrees the latitude reaches its maximum of 5 degrees.
16:11: The table covers only 10 to 90 degrees, because from 90 to 180 the values repeat in reverse order (symmetry). At 180 degrees the moon returns to a node and its latitude is zero again.
16:12: To find the latitude between two table entries (for example at 15 degrees), use interpolation: take the difference between the two nearest values and compute proportionally.
16:13: If the distance from the node is between 90 and 180 degrees, subtract it from 180 and use the result to look up the table. For example, a distance of 150 degrees is equivalent to 30 degrees for latitude purposes.
16:14: If the distance is greater than 180, subtract 180 and start the count over. The rule is: from 0 to 180 the latitude rises and falls once, and from 180 to 360 it rises and falls once again.
16:15: When the distance from the node is between 180 and 360, the direction reverses: if in the first half the moon was north of the ecliptic, in the second half it is south, and vice versa.
16:16: The practical rule: from 0 to 180 past the ascending node the moon is north of the ecliptic. From 180 to 360 past the ascending node the moon is south. The descending node is the point where the direction reverses.
16:17: Every latitude calculation requires determining two things: the magnitude of the latitude (how many degrees) and its direction (north or south). Both are needed for computing the moon's visibility.
16:18: Example: if the moon is 200 degrees from the ascending node, subtract 180 to get 20. Look up 20 in the table to get a certain latitude. The direction: since we have passed 180 (the descending node), the direction is reversed -- south.
16:19: Full example: calculate the position of the ascending node, find the distance between the moon and the node, look up the latitude in the table, and determine the direction. The result: the moon is X degrees north or south of the ecliptic.
17:1: To determine whether the new moon is visible, one must compute the ‘third longitude’ — a corrected position that accounts for parallax. What is parallax? All calculations so far assumed the observer is at the center of the earth. But the observer stands on the surface — and because the moon is relatively close, the difference in viewing angle between these two points is significant (up to nearly 1°). The diagram shows the two sight lines — from the center and from the observer — and the parallax angle between them.
17:2: Parallax arises because the observer stands on the surface of the earth, not at its center. Due to the moon's relative closeness to the earth, there is a significant difference between the calculated position and the apparent position.
17:3: The parallax correction in longitude shifts the moon's apparent position in a certain direction. The closer the moon is to the horizon, the greater the shift.
17:4: The parallax correction in latitude changes the moon's apparent latitude. Here too, the lower the moon is on the horizon, the greater the correction.
17:5: The "third longitude" is obtained after both corrections: the longitude correction and the latitude correction. This is the true apparent position of the moon as observed from the Land of Israel.
17:6: All calculations are calibrated for the Land of Israel, specifically the Jerusalem area. For other locations, the results may vary slightly.
17:7: After finding the third longitude, one computes the ‘arc of sighting’ — the angle between the setting sun and the crescent moon as seen by the observer. This is the central measure for determining visibility. The diagram shows a sunset scene: sun on the horizon, crescent above it, and the arc between them. The larger the arc, the higher the moon sits above the horizon and the easier it is to see.
17:8: Computing the arc of sighting involves the difference in longitude (east-west) and the difference in latitude (north-south) between the sun and the moon. Both factors together determine the apparent distance.
17:9: Rambam provides tables for precise calculation of parallax corrections, based on the moon's position along the zodiac and its altitude above the horizon.
17:10: The tables are calibrated for the geographic latitude of the Land of Israel (approximately 32 degrees north). In other regions, the parallax values differ.
17:11: The three stages of calculation are summarized as: First Longitude = mean position (chapters 12, 14), Second Longitude = true position after anomaly correction (chapters 13, 15), Third Longitude = apparent position after parallax correction (chapter 17). Each stage refines the previous one. The flow diagram shows the progression: Mean → correction → True → correction → Apparent.
17:12: After all corrections, two final values are obtained: the arc of sighting (sun-moon distance) and the first longitude of the moon (its position relative to the horizon). Both are needed to determine visibility.
17:13: Rambam provides a complete worked example that goes through every step of the calculation: from computing the mean position, through the corrections, to the final visibility determination.
17:14: The example shows how all the tables and rules learned in earlier chapters come together into one complete process. At the end, a definitive answer is reached: whether or not the moon is visible.
17:15: Key rule for visibility: if the arc of sighting is 9 degrees or less, the moon will certainly not be visible. If the arc is 14 degrees or more, the moon will certainly be visible everywhere. Between 9 and 14, it depends on additional factors.
17:16: To determine visibility when the arc of sighting is between 9 and 14 degrees, a threshold table is used: the arc plus the first longitude must reach at least 22 degrees for the moon to be visible.
17:17: The threshold table accounts for the fact that the moon is easier to see when it is higher above the horizon. Therefore, a larger first longitude compensates for a smaller arc of sighting.
17:18: There are special cases that require additional calculations -- for example, when the moon is very close to the visibility threshold or when its position is particularly unusual.
17:19: For each month, it suffices to calculate visibility for one evening only -- the evening of day 30 of the previous month. If the moon is visible, the new month begins. If not, one waits until day 31.
17:20: When the court was active, the calculation served to verify witness testimony. If the calculation showed the moon could not have been visible and witnesses claimed they saw it, the court knew the testimony was false.
17:21: Rambam emphasizes that even though the overall calculation is complex, each individual step is simple. The complexity arises from the number of steps, not from the difficulty of any single step.
17:22: All the steps can be organized into a single table and followed in a fixed order. This turns the calculation into a systematic process that anyone can perform.
17:23: Rambam notes that more precise calculation methods exist, but they are more complex and are not necessary for the halakhic purpose of determining the new month.
17:24: In summary: the entire calculation process -- from the mean position, through the corrections, to the visibility determination -- is aimed at answering one simple question: can the new moon be seen this evening?
18:1: Even if the calculation shows the moon should be visible, it may not actually be seen due to clouds, haze, fog, or other weather conditions. The calculation gives the theoretical potential, not the reality on the ground.
18:2: The moon is easier to see in the clear air of winter than in the dusty, hot air of summer. The season affects atmospheric transparency and visibility.
18:3: The court would take the season and weather conditions into account when evaluating testimony from witnesses who claimed to have seen the moon. Different conditions required different levels of scrutiny.
18:4: If the court knew the moon was near the visibility threshold and conditions were difficult, they would be more stringent in examining the witnesses. If conditions were favorable, they would accept testimony more readily.
18:5: Practical rule: if the moon was not seen on day 30, the previous month becomes "full" (30 days) and the new month begins on day 31. There is no need to wait any longer.
18:6: If for two consecutive months the moon was not seen on day 30, both months become full. There is no halakhic problem with this -- it simply happens from time to time as a matter of reality.
18:7: The court did not rely solely on calculation. They sent messengers to elevated observation points, and if the messengers reported a sighting, they sanctified the month.
18:8: Fundamental rule: the calculation is an auxiliary tool, but the court's final decision is based on actual eyewitness testimony. The calculation helps verify the testimony and prevent errors.
19:1: This chapter deals with the direction the new crescent faces -- whether it tilts to the right, to the left, or straight up. The court used this information to verify testimony: if a witness described a direction that did not match the calculation, the testimony was rejected.
19:2: The sun’s path (the ecliptic) is tilted at about 23.5° relative to the celestial equator. The two planes intersect at two ‘equinoctial points’ — where the sun crosses the equator (at the start of Aries and Libra). In summer the sun is north of the equator (Cancer, Leo, Virgo); in winter it is south (Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces). This tilt directly affects the direction of the crescent.
19:3: The tilt between the ecliptic and the celestial equator causes the sun's position relative to the equator to change throughout the year -- this is what creates the seasons.
19:4: Each zodiac sign has a "declination" -- its distance from the celestial equator. The summer signs (Cancer, Leo, Virgo) are north of the equator, and the winter signs (Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces) are south.
19:5: The sun's maximum declination is approximately 23.5 degrees (at the beginning of Cancer and Capricorn). At the equinoctial points (beginning of Aries and Libra) the declination is zero.
19:6: The declination affects the direction the crescent faces, because the angle at which the ecliptic intersects the horizon changes with the declination.
19:7: The declination table gives the distance from the celestial equator for every 10 degrees along the zodiac, from 0 to 90 degrees. Like the latitude table, the values are symmetrical.
19:8: Interpolation in the declination table works the same way as in the moon's latitude table: find the two nearest values and compute proportionally.
19:9: The declination determines how many degrees north or south of the celestial equator each point on the ecliptic lies. This value is needed to calculate the angle at which the crescent appears.
19:10: To find the moon's distance from the celestial equator, combine two factors: the declination of the ecliptic at the moon's position, and the moon's latitude (its distance from the ecliptic). If both are in the same direction, add them; if in opposite directions, subtract.
19:11: The final result gives the precise distance of the moon from the celestial equator. This information, combined with the sun's position, determines the direction of the crescent.
19:12: The central rule is summarized in three cases: (1) Moon on the celestial equator — horns point straight up. (2) Moon north of the equator — crescent tilts southeast, opening faces northwest. (3) Moon south of the equator — crescent tilts northeast, opening faces southwest. The diagram shows all three cases side by side.
19:13: If the moon is south of the celestial equator, the crescent tilts toward the northeast and the arc of sighting faces southwest. The farther the moon is from the equator, the greater the tilt.
19:14: The court used this information when examining witnesses: they asked which direction the crescent faced and compared it to the calculation. A mismatch indicated false testimony.
19:15: Summary of the calculation method: compute the moon's position, the sun's position, the moon's latitude, the ecliptic declination, and the distance from the celestial equator -- and from all these, derive the crescent's direction, the arc of sighting, and the visibility.
19:16: Rambam concludes with the verse: "Seek from the book of the Lord and read; not one of these shall be missing" -- meaning that all the details presented here are precise and complete, and none of them is lacking.
